report_markdown:
# Patent Eligibility Screen Report

This is a preliminary automated screen, not a legal opinion. It is not intended for patent filing, prosecution, or as legal advice. Consult qualified patent counsel for formal evaluation.

## Executive Summary

Overall determination: **LIKELY_ELIGIBLE**.
The invention falls within a patentable category and does not involve a judicial exception (abstract idea, law of nature, or natural phenomenon).
Review status: **Complete**.

## Decision Path (At a Glance)

| Stage | Eligibility Question | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | Does the invention fall within a statutory category? | Yes |
| 3 | Does the invention recite a judicial exception? | No — analysis ended |
| 4 | Does the invention integrate the exception into a practical application? | Skipped — Stage 3 answered No |
| 5 | Do the additional elements amount to significantly more than the judicial exception? | Skipped — Stage 3 answered No |

## Recommended Next Steps

Your invention appears to be eligible for patent protection. The next step is a **prior art search** to determine whether it is novel and non-obvious compared to existing patents and publications, followed by a formal **patentability opinion** from patent counsel.

## How This Report Works

This report follows the [Alice/Mayo eligibility framework](https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html#ch2100_d29a1b_139db_e0), which is the test the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO) uses to decide whether an invention is eligible for patent protection under [35 U.S.C. § 101](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/101). The framework is documented in the USPTO's [Manual of Patent Examining Procedure (MPEP), § 2106](https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html).

The test works as a series of stages. Each stage asks a yes/no question. Depending on the answer, the analysis either continues to the next stage or exits early because the eligibility question has already been resolved. When a stage is marked "Skipped," it means a prior stage already answered the eligibility question — the explanation for why is provided inline.

---

## Stage 1: Invention Extraction

Before evaluating eligibility, the system extracts key details from your disclosure. **Please verify that this matches your invention** — if the extraction is wrong, the downstream analysis will be unreliable.

- **Title**: Novel Optical Technique to Measure Depth of Focal Plane
- **Abstract**: A 3D optical calibration device consisting of a cylinder and cone structure that enables measurement of depth of focus in optical systems by comparing changing and constant radii as the imaging plane is adjusted through the sample depth.
- **Problem Solved**: Current tools for measuring depth of focus in optical systems are either expensive (interferometry systems), require constant readjustment (micrometer/piezo stages), or are difficult to access. This invention provides a simple, easily manufactured device that can be integrated into any optical setup for quick depth of focus measurement and calibration.
- **Technology Area**: Optical measurement and calibration devices
- **Claims Present**: no
- **Claims Summary**: No claims found in disclosure

### Invention Description

The invention is a 3D structure containing two geometric shapes: (1) a cylinder with constant radius throughout its height, and (2) a cone with radius that changes as a function of height at a known angle. When viewed through an optical system, users can adjust the imaging plane depth and observe two circles - one that remains constant (cylinder) and one that changes size (cone). By measuring the relative sizes of these circles and using the known cone angle, users can calculate the exact depth of the imaging plane and determine the optical system's depth of focus capabilities. The device can be manufactured in resin for prototypes or glass for final products, and integrated into standard optical measurement slides.

### Novel Elements

1. 3D structure combining cylinder and cone geometries for depth measurement
2. Use of constant radius (cylinder) as control reference against changing radius (cone)
3. Simple visual comparison method using two circles in field of view
4. Mathematical calculation of depth using known cone angle and radius measurements
5. Integration capability with existing optical measurement tools like USAF 1951 targets
6. Cost-effective alternative to expensive interferometry systems
7. Easy manufacturing in resin or glass materials

> Extraction confidence: 0.90 — The disclosure provides comprehensive technical details including design specifications, usage methodology, mathematical calculations, materials, and manufacturing approaches. The invention is clearly described with sufficient detail for implementation.

---

## Stage 2: Statutory Category

U.S. patent law ([35 U.S.C. § 101](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/101)) requires that an invention fall into one of four categories: a **process** (method), **machine**, **manufacture** (manufactured article), or **composition of matter** (chemical compound, mixture, etc.). If the invention does not fit any of these, it cannot be patented regardless of how novel it is. See [MPEP § 2106.03](https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html#ch2100_d29a1b_139db_e0).

**Question**: Does the invention fall within a statutory category?

**Conclusion**: Yes — the invention falls within a statutory category.

**Categories identified**: MANUFACTURE

**Reasoning**: The invention is a 3D physical structure containing geometric shapes (cylinder and cone) that is manufactured from materials like resin or glass. This constitutes a 'manufacture' under 35 U.S.C. § 101 as it is an article produced from raw materials (resin or glass) by giving them new forms, qualities, and properties through manufacturing processes. The device has a specific physical configuration designed for optical measurement purposes and can be integrated into standard optical measurement slides. While the invention involves a method of use (measuring depth through optical observation), the claims summary indicates no specific process claims are presented, and the core invention as described is the physical 3D structure itself.

> Confidence: 0.85 — High confidence based on clear description of a physical manufactured article with specific geometric properties and materials, fitting squarely within the manufacture category definition.

---

## Stage 3: Judicial Exception

The Supreme Court has long held that certain categories of ideas cannot be patented by themselves. The current framework for analyzing these exceptions was established in [*Mayo Collaborative Services v. Prometheus*](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/566/66/) (2012) and [*Alice Corp. v. CLS Bank*](https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/573/208/) (2014). These categories include **abstract ideas** (e.g., mathematical formulas, mental processes, methods of organizing human activity), **laws of nature**, and **natural phenomena**. These are called "judicial exceptions." This stage checks whether your invention's core concept falls into one of these categories. See [MPEP § 2106.04](https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html#ch2100_d29a1b_13c11_1cb).

**Question**: Does the invention recite a judicial exception?

**Conclusion**: No — the invention does not recite a judicial exception.

**Reasoning**: The claim describes a physical 3D structure containing geometric shapes (cylinder and cone) that is manufactured in tangible materials (resin or glass) and integrated into optical measurement slides. This is a concrete, physical device with specific structural elements that interact with optical systems to enable depth measurements. While the device may be used in conjunction with mathematical calculations or mental processes by users, the claim itself recites the physical structure and its tangible components, not the abstract concepts. The geometric relationships are embodied in the physical structure itself, not recited as abstract mathematical concepts. The claim focuses on the manufacture and structural configuration of a tangible optical measurement device rather than on abstract ideas, laws of nature, or natural phenomena.

**MPEP Reference**: [MPEP § 2106.04](https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html#ch2100_d29a1b_13c11_1cb)

> Confidence: 0.85 — High confidence as the claim clearly describes a physical manufactured device with specific structural elements, which falls outside judicial exceptions.

**What this means**: The analysis stops here — and this is good news. Because no judicial exception was identified, your invention clears the eligibility hurdle. The remaining question is whether it is novel and non-obvious compared to prior art (see the §102/§103 section below).

---

## Stage 4: Practical Application

If the invention does involve a judicial exception (an abstract idea, law of nature, etc.), it can still be patented if the claims integrate that exception into a **practical application** — meaning the invention uses the abstract idea to achieve a concrete, real-world result, not just "apply it on a computer" or "use it in a general way." See [MPEP § 2106.04(d)](https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html#ch2100_d29a1b_2117e_1e5).

**Question**: Does the invention integrate the judicial exception into a practical application?

**Conclusion**: Not evaluated — analysis ended at Stage 3.

Stage 3 found that your invention does **not** involve a judicial exception (abstract idea, law of nature, or natural phenomenon). Since there is no exception to "integrate," this stage is not needed. Your invention already cleared the eligibility hurdle at Stage 3.


---

## Stage 5: Inventive Concept

This stage is only reached if the invention involves a judicial exception (Stage 3) and does **not** integrate it into a practical application (Stage 4). It asks whether the invention nonetheless has an "inventive concept" — something beyond routine or conventional use of the abstract idea — that makes it patent-eligible. See [MPEP § 2106.05](https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html#ch2100_d29a1b_21506_344).

The [Berkheimer Memo](https://www.uspto.gov/sites/default/files/documents/memo-berkheimer-20180419.pdf) (April 2018) requires that any finding of well-understood, routine, or conventional activity must be supported by evidence — an examiner cannot simply assert it. See [MPEP § 2106.05(d)](https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html#ch2100_d29a1b_21506_344).

**Question**: Do the additional elements amount to significantly more than the judicial exception?

**Conclusion**: Not evaluated — analysis ended at Stage 3.

This stage was not needed because Stage 3 found no judicial exception. Your invention already cleared the eligibility hurdle.


---

## Prior Art Advisory Flags

This section is separate from the eligibility analysis above. Even if an invention is eligible for patenting, it must also be **novel** (not already known — [35 U.S.C. § 102](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/102)) and **non-obvious** (not an obvious combination of known ideas — [35 U.S.C. § 103](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/103)). The flags below are preliminary concerns based on the disclosure text alone, without a formal prior art search.

**Prior Art Search Priority**: `HIGH`

### Novelty Concerns (§ 102)

- Optical test targets with geometric shapes are well-established in the field
- Depth measurement using optical systems has extensive prior art
- Cylindrical and conical test objects are known in optical metrology
- Visual comparison methods for optical measurements are conventional
- Standard optical slides with test patterns are widely available
- Mathematical calculations using geometric relationships are routine

### Non-Obviousness Concerns (§ 103)

- Combining cylinder and cone in single structure may be obvious to skilled artisan
- Using constant reference against variable element is conventional approach
- Simple geometric calculations for depth measurement would be expected
- Integration with existing optical tools follows standard practice
- Manufacturing in resin/glass materials is routine for optical components

**Reasoning**: This invention operates in the well-established field of optical measurement and calibration devices, where extensive prior art exists. The core concept involves basic geometric shapes (cylinder and cone) used for optical depth measurement, which are fundamental elements in optical metrology. The visual comparison method and mathematical calculations using geometric relationships are conventional approaches. The manufacturing materials (resin/glass) and integration with standard optical slides are routine in the industry. Given the straightforward nature of the geometric approach and the mature field of optical measurement, there is significant likelihood that similar devices or methods exist in the prior art. The combination of well-known elements (cylinder, cone, optical measurement) for a predictable result (depth measurement) suggests potential obviousness issues.

> Confidence: 0.80 — High confidence based on mature optical measurement field and conventional geometric approaches
---

## Appendix

### Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
|------|--------|
| [§ 101](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/101) | The patent statute requiring inventions be useful, novel, and fall within eligible categories |
| [§ 102](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/102) | The novelty requirement — the invention must not already be publicly known |
| [§ 103](https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/35/103) | The non-obviousness requirement — the invention must not be an obvious variation of known ideas |
| [Alice/Mayo](https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html#ch2100_d29a1b_139db_e0) | Supreme Court cases establishing the two-step test for patent eligibility |
| [MPEP](https://www.uspto.gov/web/offices/pac/mpep/s2106.html) | Manual of Patent Examining Procedure — the USPTO examiner's guidebook |
| Judicial exception | An abstract idea, law of nature, or natural phenomenon that cannot be patented by itself |
| Practical application | Using an abstract idea in a specific, concrete way that goes beyond the idea itself |
| Inventive concept | An element that transforms an abstract idea into something significantly more than the idea alone |
| [Berkheimer Memo](https://www.uspto.gov/sites/default/files/documents/memo-berkheimer-20180419.pdf) | USPTO guidance requiring evidence for any finding that claim elements are well-understood, routine, and conventional (WURC) |
| Well-understood, routine, and conventional (WURC) | Claim elements that examiners assert are standard in the field and, under Berkheimer, must be supported by evidence |
| Statutory category | The four § 101 categories: process, machine, manufacture, or composition of matter |
| Prior art | Existing patents, publications, or public knowledge that an invention is compared against |
